section.title: Friendship and Foresight
section.sectionRationale: Continuing the Hezekiah collection, 25:1 to 29:27, this chapter gathers proverbs on humility about tomorrow, the honest wounds of a true friend versus the false kisses of an enemy, and the foresight of a shepherd who knows the state of his flock before riches themselves prove fleeting.
section.thesis: Wisdom refuses to boast about tomorrow, welcomes the painful honesty of a true friend over the flattering kisses of an enemy, and tends carefully to what actually sustains life, since riches do not last forever but a well-known flock does.
section.frameNote: Four clusters: do not boast about tomorrow, and let another praise you; the wounds of a friend and the sweetness of counsel; the never-satisfied eye, and iron sharpening iron; know the state of your flocks, for riches are not forever.

verses.text: Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring.
Let another praise you, and not your own mouth, a stranger, and not your own lips.
A stone is heavy and sand is a burden, but aggravation from a fool outweighs them both.
Wrath is cruel and anger is like a flood, but who can withstand jealousy?
Better an open rebuke than love that is concealed.
The wounds of a {{ohev|friend}} are faithful, but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful.
The soul that is full loathes honey, but to a hungry soul, any bitter thing is sweet.
Like a bird that strays from its nest is a man who wanders from his home.
Oil and incense bring joy to the heart, and the sweetness of a friend is counsel to the soul.
Do not forsake your friend or your father's friend, and do not go to your brother's house in the day of your calamity; better a neighbor nearby than a brother far away.
Be wise, my son, and bring joy to my heart, so that I can answer him who taunts me.
The prudent see danger and take cover; but the simple keep going and pay the penalty.
Take the garment of him who posts security for a stranger; get collateral if it is for a foreigner.
If one blesses his neighbor with a loud voice early in the morning, it will be counted to him as a curse.
A constant dripping on a rainy day and a contentious woman are alike,
restraining her is like holding back the wind or grasping oil with one's right hand.
As iron sharpens iron, so one man sharpens another.
Whoever tends a fig tree will eat its fruit, and he who looks after his master will be honored.
As water reflects the face, so the heart reflects the true man.
Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied; so the eyes of man are never satisfied.
A crucible for silver and a furnace for gold, but a man is tested by the praise accorded him.
Though you grind a fool like grain with mortar and a pestle, yet his folly will not depart from him.

units.label: Know the state of your flocks

verses.text: Be sure to know the state of your flocks, and pay close attention to your herds;
for riches are not forever, nor does a crown endure to every generation.
When hay is removed and new growth appears and the grain from the hills is gathered,
the lambs will provide you with clothing, and the goats with the price of a field.
You will have plenty of goats' milk to feed you, food for your household and nourishment for your maidservants.

ohev.gloss: One who loves, a true friend. Verse 6 pairs the wounds of such a friend, painful but faithful, against the kisses of an enemy, pleasant but deceitful, teaching that real love sometimes hurts on the surface while flattery, however sweet, betrays underneath.

basic.context: This chapter mixes practical wisdom about humility, friendship, and foresight. It warns against boasting about a future no one controls, praises the painful honesty of a true friend over an enemy's flattering kiss, and closes with the down to earth counsel to know your flocks well, since riches, unlike careful stewardship, never last forever.
basic.hermeneutics: These proverbs describe how wise living generally works: humility about the future, welcoming honest correction, cultivating real friendship, and diligent stewardship of what sustains life. They are not formulas guaranteeing specific outcomes but patterns that consistently mark a wise life under God.
basic.originalLanguages: Ohev, one who loves, verse 6, names the true friend whose wounds are faithful even when they hurt, set against the kisses of an enemy, which flatter yet deceive. The chapter's repeated agricultural images, tending a fig tree, verse 18, knowing the state of flocks, verse 23, reflect the everyday rural world of ancient Israel where careful stewardship, not sudden wealth, secured a family's future.
basic.typology: The faithful wounds of a friend, verse 6, anticipate the piercing yet saving wounds of Christ, by whose stripes His people are healed, Isaiah 53:5. Iron sharpening iron, verse 17, foreshadows the mutual sanctification believers are called to in the body of Christ, spurring one another on toward love and good deeds, Hebrews 10:24.
basic.christ: Verse 6's faithful wounds of a friend point ultimately to Christ, whose wounds, unlike any merely human friend's, were not just faithful but redemptive, by His stripes we are healed, Isaiah 53:5, the deepest possible fulfillment of a friend's painful love doing us good. And verse 17's picture of iron sharpening iron anticipates the mutual sharpening believers are called to within the church, the body of Christ, spurring one another on to love and good works as together they are conformed to Him, Hebrews 10:24-25.
basic.god: God stands behind the warning against boasting about tomorrow, verse 1, since only He truly knows and holds the future securely. He also stands behind the deeper testing pictured in verse 21, where a crucible refines silver, but a person is refined by how he handles praise, showing that God cares as much about character under flattery as character under trial.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53:5 fulfills the faithful wounds of a friend in verse 6. Hebrews 10:24-25 develops the mutual sharpening of iron in verse 17. James 4:13-16 echoes the warning against boasting about tomorrow in verse 1. Galatians 6:2 echoes the friendship and mutual burden-bearing commended throughout the chapter.
basic.oneStory: Proverbs 27 calls for humility about the future, honest friendship over flattering deception, and diligent care for what actually sustains life. This whole vision reaches its height in Christ, whose faithful wounds heal rather than merely correct, and in the church, His body, where believers sharpen one another into His likeness.

context.literary: Chapter 27 belongs to the Hezekiah collection, 25:1 to 29:27, gathering proverbs loosely around humility, friendship, and stewardship, and closing with an extended, almost pastoral meditation on flocks and herds that stands out for its sustained agricultural focus.
context.historical: In an agrarian economy where family wealth was measured in flocks, herds, and land rather than liquid currency, the counsel to 'know the state of your flocks,' verse 23, was not merely sentimental but a matter of literal survival, since inherited wealth, unlike careful ongoing stewardship, could vanish within a generation.
context.canonical: Verse 17's image of iron sharpening iron has become one of the most widely quoted pictures of Christian community and mutual discipleship, echoed in the New Testament's repeated 'one another' commands for believers within the church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To cultivate humility about an uncertain future, to commend the painful honesty of true friendship over flattering deception, and to urge diligent stewardship of tangible resources rather than presumption on unearned or inherited wealth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 1, 2, and 23 prescribe conduct directly, do not boast, let another praise you, know your flocks, while verses like 20, concerning the never-satisfied human eye, describe a persistent feature of human nature more than issuing a command, functioning as diagnosis that should provoke humility and contentment.

debates.question: Does verse 6, 'better an open rebuke than love that is concealed,' teach that all criticism, however delivered, should be welcomed as a mark of true friendship?

views.label: Any rebuke, however harsh, proves greater love than silence
views.case: The stark contrast between a friend's wounds and an enemy's kisses could be read as valuing bluntness itself, regardless of tone or manner, over any withheld criticism.

views.label: The verse commends honest, well-meant correction motivated by love, not harshness for its own sake
views.case: The Hebrew parallel is between 'faithful' wounds, verse 6, meaning trustworthy and well-intentioned, and mere flattery; Proverbs elsewhere prizes gentle, well-timed speech, 25:11 and 15:1, so the point is honesty rooted in genuine care, not license for cruelty dressed up as friendship.

debates.bearing: Read alongside the rest of Proverbs' concern for gentle, well-timed speech, the proverb commends honest correction offered out of real love, not harshness excused as honesty; the test is the friend's faithfulness and motive, not merely the sting of the words.

study.originalLanguages: Ohev, one who loves, verse 6, names a friend whose painful honesty is called 'faithful,' trustworthy and well-meant, set directly against the deceptive kisses of an enemy, a vivid Hebrew contrast between substance and surface. The chapter's closing section leans on concrete agrarian vocabulary, flocks, herds, hay, and grain, reflecting the literal, tangible basis of household wealth in ancient Israel.

typology.type: The faithful wounds of a friend, 27:6
typology.fulfillment: The piercing yet healing wounds of Christ, by whose stripes we are healed, Isaiah 53:5.
Mutual sanctification within the body of Christ, spurring one another to love and good deeds, Hebrews 10:24-25.

study.christ: The faithful wounds of a true friend, verse 6, however painful, find their deepest fulfillment in Christ, whose wounds were not merely honest correction but redemptive suffering, for by His stripes we are healed, Isaiah 53:5, the friend whose pain purchased our peace. And verse 17's picture of iron sharpening iron anticipates the New Testament's vision of the church as a community where believers sharpen one another, sometimes through loving confrontation, toward greater conformity to Christ, spurring one another on to love and good works, Hebrews 10:24-25.
study.god: God alone truly knows and holds the future that no person should presume to boast about, verse 1, and He values the deeper testing of character under praise as much as under adversity, verse 21. He is the giver of the tangible provision this chapter closes with, flocks, herds, milk, and clothing, reminding His people that ongoing dependence on Him, not presumption on inherited wealth, sustains a household.

crossReferences.note: By His stripes we are healed, the ultimate fulfillment of the faithful wounds of a friend in Proverbs 27:6.
Spur one another on to love and good deeds, developing iron sharpening iron in Proverbs 27:17.
You do not know what tomorrow will bring, echoing the warning of Proverbs 27:1.
Carry each other's burdens, echoing the friendship commended throughout Proverbs 27.

study.oneStory: Proverbs 27 calls for humility about an uncertain future, honest and faithful friendship over flattering deceit, and diligent stewardship of what actually sustains a household. This entire vision comes to its fullest expression in Christ, whose wounds heal rather than merely wound, and in His church, where believers sharpen one another into His likeness.

sermon.title: Friendship
sermon.why: Preached at Redeemer Presbyterian Church on May 29, 2005 in the series Proverbs: True Wisdom for Living, this sermon works directly from Proverbs 27:5-6, 9, 14, and 17, among other friendship texts, describing the marks of a true friend, constancy, carefulness, candor, and counsel, and grounding the capacity for real friendship in the friendship of Jesus shown at the cross.

options.text: Boasting about tomorrow, since you do not know what a day may bring.
options.note: The uncertainty of the future should produce humility, not presumptuous boasting.

options.text: Making any plans at all for the future.
options.note: The verse warns against boastful presumption, not planning itself.

options.text: Speaking about the past.
options.note: The verse's concern is tomorrow, not the past.

options.text: Sharing your plans with close friends.
options.note: The warning is against arrogant boasting, not simply discussing plans with trusted friends.

quiz.q: What does verse 6 say about the wounds of a friend compared to the kisses of an enemy?

options.text: The friend's wounds are faithful, but the enemy's kisses are deceitful.
options.note: Painful honesty from someone who loves you is more trustworthy than pleasant flattery from someone who does not.

options.text: Both are equally trustworthy expressions of care.
options.note: The verse draws a sharp contrast, not an equivalence.

options.text: The enemy's kisses are more valuable since they are pleasant.
options.note: This reverses the verse's clear point.

options.text: Wounds from anyone, friend or enemy, should always be trusted.
options.note: The verse specifically distinguishes a friend's faithful wounds from an enemy's deceitful kisses, not wounds from any source.

quiz.q: What does verse 17 say about how people affect one another?

options.text: As iron sharpens iron, so one man sharpens another.
options.note: Close relationships can refine and improve character through mutual influence.

options.text: People have no real effect on one another's character.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's point.

options.text: Iron is used only as a metaphor for wealth in this verse.
options.note: The metaphor is about sharpening and mutual influence, not wealth.

options.text: Only teachers can sharpen students, not peers.
options.note: The verse's image, one man sharpening another, suggests mutual peer influence, not a one-directional teacher-student relationship exclusively.

quiz.q: What does verse 20 say about human eyes?

options.text: Like Sheol and Abaddon, they are never satisfied.
options.note: Human desire is pictured as insatiable, like the realm of the dead that never has enough.

options.text: They are easily satisfied with modest possessions.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's claim.

options.text: They are satisfied only by great wealth.
options.note: The verse suggests an inherent insatiability, not a threshold that, once reached, brings satisfaction.

options.text: They become more easily satisfied with age.
options.note: The verse makes no claim about satisfaction changing with age; the point is a persistent, ongoing lack of satisfaction.

quiz.q: Why does verse 23-24 urge knowing the state of your flocks?

options.text: Because riches are not forever, nor does a crown endure to every generation.
options.note: Diligent stewardship matters precisely because wealth is not automatically permanent.

options.text: Because flocks are more valuable than money.
options.note: The comparison is about permanence and diligence, not relative value of assets.

options.text: Because riches, once gained, last forever automatically.
options.note: This is the opposite of what the text explicitly states.

options.text: Because it is a purely sentimental, non-practical suggestion.
options.note: The passage frames this as practical wisdom tied to real economic survival, not mere sentiment.

questions: Verse 1 warns against boasting about tomorrow. Where have you been presuming on a future that is not actually guaranteed to you?
Verse 6 says a friend's wounds are more trustworthy than an enemy's kisses. Who in your life loves you enough to tell you a hard truth, and how do you usually respond when they do?
Iron sharpens iron, verse 17. Who is sharpening you right now, and who are you being called to sharpen?
Verse 20 says human eyes are never satisfied. Where in your own life is more never actually enough, and what would contentment look like there?

Verse 23 calls for knowing the state of your flocks rather than presuming on wealth. What area of your life, finances, health, relationships, needs more attentive, ongoing care rather than passive assumption?

translationNotes.note: 'Faithful' translates ne'emanim, the same root behind 'amen,' meaning trustworthy, reliable, firm, contrasting sharply with the deceit of an enemy's kiss.
'Grasping oil with one's right hand' pictures something inherently ungraspable, oil slipping through fingers, applied here to the futility of trying to restrain a contentious spirit by force.
'Sheol and Abaddon' name the realm and the place of destruction associated with death, both personified here as insatiable, paralleling the endless appetite of human desire.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 27. Sermon link verified this session via web search and direct page fetch of podcast.gospelinlife.com. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
